8.play: DUKE OF BUCKINGHAM

12.play: EARL OF WARWICK

17.play: VAUX

18.play: MATTHEW GOFFE, A Sea-Captain, Master, and Master's-Mate, and Walter Whitmore

28.play: MAYOR OF SAINT ALBAN'S

29.play: GEORGE BEVIS, John Holland, Dick the butcher, Smith the weaver, &c., followers of Cade

31.play: MARGARET, Queen to King Henry

32.play: MARGARET JOURDAIN, a witch

33.play: ELEANOR, Duchess of Gloucester, Wife to Simpcox

1.play: [Flourish of trumpets: then hautboys. Enter KING  HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER, SALISBURY, WARWICK, and  CARDINAL, on the one side; QUEEN MARGARET, SUFFOLK, YORK, SOMERSET, and BUCKINGHAM, on the other]

2.play: SUFFOLK: As by your high imperial majesty

3.play: I had in charge at my depart for France,

4.play: As procurator to your excellence,

5.play: To marry Princess Margaret for your grace,

6.play: So, in the famous ancient city, Tours,

7.play: In presence of the Kings of France and Sicil,

8.play: The Dukes of Orleans, Calaber, Bretagne and Alencon,

9.play: Seven earls, twelve barons and twenty reverend bishops,

10.play: I have perform'd my task and was espoused:

11.play: And humbly now upon my bended knee,

12.play: In sight of England and her lordly peers,

13.play: Deliver up my title in the queen

14.play: To your most gracious hands, that are the substance

15.play: Of that great shadow I did represent;

16.play: The happiest gift that ever marquess gave,

17.play: The fairest queen that ever king received.

18.play: KING HENRY VI: Suffolk, arise. Welcome, Queen Margaret:

19.play: I can express no kinder sign of love

20.play: Than this kind kiss. O Lord, that lends me life,

21.play: Lend me a heart replete with thankfulness!

22.play: For thou hast given me in this beauteous face

23.play: A world of earthly blessings to my soul,

24.play: If sympathy of love unite our thoughts.

25.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Great King of England and my gracious lord,

26.play: The mutual conference that my mind hath had,

27.play: By day, by night, waking and in my dreams,

28.play: In courtly company or at my beads,

29.play: With you, mine alder-liefest sovereign,

30.play: Makes me the bolder to salute my king

31.play: With ruder terms, such as my wit affords

32.play: And over-joy of heart doth minister.

33.play: KING HENRY VI: Her sight did ravish; but her grace in speech,

34.play: Her words y-clad with wisdom's majesty,

35.play: Makes me from wondering fall to weeping joys;

36.play: Such is the fulness of my heart's content.

37.play: Lords, with one cheerful voice welcome my love.

38.play: ALL: [Kneeling]  Long live Queen Margaret, England's

39.play: happiness!

40.play: QUEEN MARGARET: We thank you all.

41.play: SUFFOLK: My lord protector, so it please your grace,

42.play: Here are the articles of contracted peace

43.play: Between our sovereign and the French king Charles,

44.play: For eighteen months concluded by consent.

45.play: GLOUCESTER: [Reads]  'Imprimis, it is agreed between the French

46.play: king Charles, and William de la Pole, Marquess of

47.play: Suffolk, ambassador for Henry King of England, that

48.play: the said Henry shall espouse the Lady Margaret,

49.play: daughter unto Reignier King of Naples, Sicilia and

50.play: Jerusalem, and crown her Queen of England ere the

51.play: thirtieth of May next ensuing. Item, that the duchy

52.play: of Anjou and the county of Maine shall be released

53.play: and delivered to the king her father'--

54.play: KING HENRY VI: Uncle, how now!

56.play: Some sudden qualm hath struck me at the heart

57.play: And dimm'd mine eyes, that I can read no further.

58.play: KING HENRY VI: Uncle of Winchester, I pray, read on.

59.play: CARDINAL: [Reads]  'Item, It is further agreed between them,

60.play: that the duchies of Anjou and Maine shall be

61.play: released and delivered over to the king her father,

62.play: and she sent over of the King of England's own

63.play: proper cost and charges, without having any dowry.'

64.play: KING HENRY VI: They please us well. Lord marquess, kneel down:

65.play: We here create thee the first duke of Suffolk,

66.play: And gird thee with the sword. Cousin of York,

67.play: We here discharge your grace from being regent

68.play: I' the parts of France, till term of eighteen months

69.play: Be full expired. Thanks, uncle Winchester,

72.play: We thank you all for the great favour done,

73.play: In entertainment to my princely queen.

74.play: Come, let us in, and with all speed provide

75.play: To see her coronation be perform'd.

76.play: GLOUCESTER: Brave peers of England, pillars of the state,

77.play: To you Duke Humphrey must unload his grief,

78.play: Your grief, the common grief of all the land.

79.play: What! did my brother Henry spend his youth,

80.play: His valour, coin and people, in the wars?

81.play: Did he so often lodge in open field,

82.play: In winter's cold and summer's parching heat,

83.play: To conquer France, his true inheritance?

84.play: And did my brother Bedford toil his wits,

85.play: To keep by policy what Henry got?

87.play: Brave York, Salisbury, and victorious Warwick,

88.play: Received deep scars in France and Normandy?

89.play: Or hath mine uncle Beaufort and myself,

90.play: With all the learned council of the realm,

91.play: Studied so long, sat in the council-house

92.play: Early and late, debating to and fro

93.play: How France and Frenchmen might be kept in awe,

94.play: And had his highness in his infancy

95.play: Crowned in Paris in despite of foes?

96.play: And shall these labours and these honours die?

98.play: Your deeds of war and all our counsel die?

99.play: O peers of England, shameful is this league!

100.play: Fatal this marriage, cancelling your fame,

101.play: Blotting your names from books of memory,

102.play: Razing the characters of your renown,

104.play: Undoing all, as all had never been!

105.play: CARDINAL: Nephew, what means this passionate discourse,

107.play: For France, 'tis ours; and we will keep it still.

108.play: GLOUCESTER: Ay, uncle, we will keep it, if we can;

109.play: But now it is impossible we should:

110.play: Suffolk, the new-made duke that rules the roast,

111.play: Hath given the duchy of Anjou and Maine

112.play: Unto the poor King Reignier, whose large style

113.play: Agrees not with the leanness of his purse.

114.play: SALISBURY: Now, by the death of Him that died for all,

115.play: These counties were the keys of Normandy.

116.play: But wherefore weeps Warwick, my valiant son?

117.play: WARWICK: For grief that they are past recovery:

118.play: For, were there hope to conquer them again,

119.play: My sword should shed hot blood, mine eyes no tears.

120.play: Anjou and Maine! myself did win them both;

121.play: Those provinces these arms of mine did conquer:

122.play: And are the cities, that I got with wounds,

123.play: Delivered up again with peaceful words?

125.play: YORK: For Suffolk's duke, may he be suffocate,

126.play: That dims the honour of this warlike isle!

127.play: France should have torn and rent my very heart,

128.play: Before I would have yielded to this league.

129.play: I never read but England's kings have had

130.play: Large sums of gold and dowries with their wives:

131.play: And our King Henry gives away his own,

132.play: To match with her that brings no vantages.

133.play: GLOUCESTER: A proper jest, and never heard before,

134.play: That Suffolk should demand a whole fifteenth

135.play: For costs and charges in transporting her!

136.play: She should have stayed in France and starved

138.play: CARDINAL: My Lord of Gloucester, now ye grow too hot:

139.play: It was the pleasure of my lord the King.

140.play: GLOUCESTER: My Lord of Winchester, I know your mind;

141.play: 'Tis not my speeches that you do mislike,

142.play: But 'tis my presence that doth trouble ye.

143.play: Rancour will out: proud prelate, in thy face

144.play: I see thy fury: if I longer stay,

145.play: We shall begin our ancient bickerings.

146.play: Lordings, farewell; and say, when I am gone,

147.play: I prophesied France will be lost ere long.

148.play: CARDINAL: So, there goes our protector in a rage.

149.play: 'Tis known to you he is mine enemy,

150.play: Nay, more, an enemy unto you all,

151.play: And no great friend, I fear me, to the king.

152.play: Consider, lords, he is the next of blood,

153.play: And heir apparent to the English crown:

154.play: Had Henry got an empire by his marriage,

155.play: And all the wealthy kingdoms of the west,

156.play: There's reason he should be displeased at it.

157.play: Look to it, lords! let not his smoothing words

158.play: Bewitch your hearts; be wise and circumspect.

159.play: What though the common people favour him,

160.play: Calling him 'Humphrey, the good Duke of

162.play: Clapping their hands, and crying with loud voice,

164.play: With 'God preserve the good Duke Humphrey!'

165.play: I fear me, lords, for all this flattering gloss,

166.play: He will be found a dangerous protector.

167.play: BUCKINGHAM: Why should he, then, protect our sovereign,

168.play: He being of age to govern of himself?

169.play: Cousin of Somerset, join you with me,

170.play: And all together, with the Duke of Suffolk,

171.play: We'll quickly hoise Duke Humphrey from his seat.

172.play: CARDINAL: This weighty business will not brook delay:

173.play: I'll to the Duke of Suffolk presently.

174.play: SOMERSET: Cousin of Buckingham, though Humphrey's pride

175.play: And greatness of his place be grief to us,

176.play: Yet let us watch the haughty cardinal:

178.play: Than all the princes in the land beside:

179.play: If Gloucester be displaced, he'll be protector.

180.play: BUCKINGHAM: Or thou or I, Somerset, will be protector,

181.play: Despite Duke Humphrey or the cardinal.

182.play: SALISBURY: Pride went before, ambition follows him.

183.play: While these do labour for their own preferment,

184.play: Behoves it us to labour for the realm.

185.play: I never saw but Humphrey Duke of Gloucester

186.play: Did bear him like a noble gentleman.

187.play: Oft have I seen the haughty cardinal,

188.play: More like a soldier than a man o' the church,

189.play: As stout and proud as he were lord of all,

190.play: Swear like a ruffian and demean himself

191.play: Unlike the ruler of a commonweal.

192.play: Warwick, my son, the comfort of my age,

193.play: Thy deeds, thy plainness and thy housekeeping,

194.play: Hath won the greatest favour of the commons,

195.play: Excepting none but good Duke Humphrey:

196.play: And, brother York, thy acts in Ireland,

197.play: In bringing them to civil discipline,

198.play: Thy late exploits done in the heart of France,

199.play: When thou wert regent for our sovereign,

200.play: Have made thee fear'd and honour'd of the people:

201.play: Join we together, for the public good,

202.play: In what we can, to bridle and suppress

203.play: The pride of Suffolk and the cardinal,

205.play: And, as we may, cherish Duke Humphrey's deeds,

206.play: While they do tend the profit of the land.

207.play: WARWICK: So God help Warwick, as he loves the land,

208.play: And common profit of his country!

209.play: YORK: [Aside]  And so says York, for he hath greatest cause.

210.play: SALISBURY: Then let's make haste away, and look unto the main.

211.play: WARWICK: Unto the main! O father, Maine is lost;

212.play: That Maine which by main force Warwick did win,

213.play: And would have kept so long as breath did last!

214.play: Main chance, father, you meant; but I meant Maine,

215.play: Which I will win from France, or else be slain,

216.play: YORK: Anjou and Maine are given to the French;

217.play: Paris is lost; the state of Normandy

218.play: Stands on a tickle point, now they are gone:

220.play: The peers agreed, and Henry was well pleased

221.play: To change two dukedoms for a duke's fair daughter.

222.play: I cannot blame them all: what is't to them?

223.play: 'Tis thine they give away, and not their own.

224.play: Pirates may make cheap pennyworths of their pillage

225.play: And purchase friends and give to courtezans,

226.play: Still revelling like lords till all be gone;

227.play: While as the silly owner of the goods

228.play: Weeps over them and wrings his hapless hands

229.play: And shakes his head and trembling stands aloof,

230.play: While all is shared and all is borne away,

231.play: Ready to starve and dare not touch his own:

232.play: So York must sit and fret and bite his tongue,

233.play: While his own lands are bargain'd for and sold.

234.play: Methinks the realms of England, France and Ireland

235.play: Bear that proportion to my flesh and blood

236.play: As did the fatal brand Althaea burn'd

237.play: Unto the prince's heart of Calydon.

238.play: Anjou and Maine both given unto the French!

239.play: Cold news for me, for I had hope of France,

240.play: Even as I have of fertile England's soil.

241.play: A day will come when York shall claim his own;

242.play: And therefore I will take the Nevils' parts

243.play: And make a show of love to proud Duke Humphrey,

244.play: And, when I spy advantage, claim the crown,

245.play: For that's the golden mark I seek to hit:

246.play: Nor shall proud Lancaster usurp my right,

247.play: Nor hold the sceptre in his childish fist,

248.play: Nor wear the diadem upon his head,

249.play: Whose church-like humours fits not for a crown.

250.play: Then, York, be still awhile, till time do serve:

251.play: Watch thou and wake when others be asleep,

252.play: To pry into the secrets of the state;

253.play: Till Henry, surfeiting in joys of love,

254.play: With his new bride and England's dear-bought queen,

255.play: And Humphrey with the peers be fall'n at jars:

256.play: Then will I raise aloft the milk-white rose,

257.play: With whose sweet smell the air shall be perfumed;

258.play: And in my standard bear the arms of York

259.play: To grapple with the house of Lancaster;

260.play: And, force perforce, I'll make him yield the crown,

261.play: Whose bookish rule hath pull'd fair England down.

1.play: [Enter GLOUCESTER and his DUCHESS]

2.play: DUCHESS: Why droops my lord, like over-ripen'd corn,

3.play: Hanging the head at Ceres' plenteous load?

4.play: Why doth the great Duke Humphrey knit his brows,

5.play: As frowning at the favours of the world?

6.play: Why are thine eyes fixed to the sullen earth,

7.play: Gazing on that which seems to dim thy sight?

8.play: What seest thou there? King Henry's diadem,

9.play: Enchased with all the honours of the world?

10.play: If so, gaze on, and grovel on thy face,

11.play: Until thy head be circled with the same.

12.play: Put forth thy hand, reach at the glorious gold.

13.play: What, is't too short? I'll lengthen it with mine:

14.play: And, having both together heaved it up,

15.play: We'll both together lift our heads to heaven,

16.play: And never more abase our sight so low

17.play: As to vouchsafe one glance unto the ground.

18.play: GLOUCESTER: O Nell, sweet Nell, if thou dost love thy lord,

19.play: Banish the canker of ambitious thoughts.

20.play: And may that thought, when I imagine ill

21.play: Against my king and nephew, virtuous Henry,

22.play: Be my last breathing in this mortal world!

23.play: My troublous dream this night doth make me sad.

24.play: DUCHESS: What dream'd my lord? tell me, and I'll requite it

25.play: With sweet rehearsal of my morning's dream.

26.play: GLOUCESTER: Methought this staff, mine office-badge in court,

27.play: Was broke in twain; by whom I have forgot,

28.play: But, as I think, it was by the cardinal;

29.play: And on the pieces of the broken wand

30.play: Were placed the heads of Edmund Duke of Somerset,

31.play: And William de la Pole, first duke of Suffolk.

32.play: This was my dream: what it doth bode, God knows.

33.play: DUCHESS: Tut, this was nothing but an argument

34.play: That he that breaks a stick of Gloucester's grove

35.play: Shall lose his head for his presumption.

36.play: But list to me, my Humphrey, my sweet duke:

37.play: Methought I sat in seat of majesty

38.play: In the cathedral church of Westminster,

39.play: And in that chair where kings and queens are crown'd;

40.play: Where Henry and dame Margaret kneel'd to me

41.play: And on my head did set the diadem.

42.play: GLOUCESTER: Nay, Eleanor, then must I chide outright:

44.play: Art thou not second woman in the realm,

45.play: And the protector's wife, beloved of him?

46.play: Hast thou not worldly pleasure at command,

47.play: Above the reach or compass of thy thought?

48.play: And wilt thou still be hammering treachery,

49.play: To tumble down thy husband and thyself

50.play: From top of honour to disgrace's feet?

51.play: Away from me, and let me hear no more!

52.play: DUCHESS: What, what, my lord! are you so choleric

53.play: With Eleanor, for telling but her dream?

54.play: Next time I'll keep my dreams unto myself,

56.play: GLOUCESTER: Nay, be not angry; I am pleased again.

57.play: I go. Come, Nell, thou wilt ride with us?

58.play: DUCHESS: Yes, my good lord, I'll follow presently.

59.play: Follow I must; I cannot go before,

60.play: While Gloucester bears this base and humble mind.

61.play: Were I a man, a duke, and next of blood,

62.play: I would remove these tedious stumbling-blocks

63.play: And smooth my way upon their headless necks;

64.play: And, being a woman, I will not be slack

65.play: To play my part in Fortune's pageant.

66.play: Where are you there? Sir John! nay, fear not, man,

67.play: We are alone; here's none but thee and I.

68.play: HUME: Jesus preserve your royal majesty!

69.play: DUCHESS: What say'st thou? majesty! I am but grace.

70.play: HUME: But, by the grace of God, and Hume's advice,

71.play: Your grace's title shall be multiplied.

72.play: DUCHESS: What say'st thou, man? hast thou as yet conferr'd

73.play: With Margery Jourdain, the cunning witch,

75.play: And will they undertake to do me good?

76.play: HUME: This they have promised, to show your highness

77.play: A spirit raised from depth of under-ground,

78.play: That shall make answer to such questions

79.play: As by your grace shall be propounded him.

80.play: DUCHESS: It is enough; I'll think upon the questions:

81.play: When from St. Alban's we do make return,

82.play: We'll see these things effected to the full.

83.play: Here, Hume, take this reward; make merry, man,

84.play: With thy confederates in this weighty cause.

85.play: HUME: Hume must make merry with the duchess' gold;

86.play: Marry, and shall. But how now, Sir John Hume!

87.play: Seal up your lips, and give no words but mum:

89.play: Dame Eleanor gives gold to bring the witch:

90.play: Gold cannot come amiss, were she a devil.

91.play: Yet have I gold flies from another coast;

92.play: I dare not say, from the rich cardinal

93.play: And from the great and new-made Duke of Suffolk,

94.play: Yet I do find it so; for to be plain,

95.play: They, knowing Dame Eleanor's aspiring humour,

96.play: Have hired me to undermine the duchess

97.play: And buz these conjurations in her brain.

98.play: They say 'A crafty knave does need no broker;'

99.play: Yet am I Suffolk and the cardinal's broker.

100.play: Hume, if you take not heed, you shall go near

101.play: To call them both a pair of crafty knaves.

102.play: Well, so it stands; and thus, I fear, at last

103.play: Hume's knavery will be the duchess' wreck,

104.play: And her attainture will be Humphrey's fall:

105.play: Sort how it will, I shall have gold for all.

1.play: [Enter three or four Petitioners, PETER, the Armourer's man, being one]

2.play: [Enter SUFFOLK and QUEEN MARGARET]

3.play: PETER: Here a' comes, methinks, and the queen with him.

4.play: I'll be the first, sure.

5.play: SUFFOLK: How now, fellow! would'st anything with me?

6.play: QUEEN MARGARET: [Reading]  'To my Lord Protector!' Are your

7.play: supplications to his lordship? Let me see them:

8.play: what is thine?

9.play: SUFFOLK: Thy wife, too! that's some wrong, indeed. What's

10.play: yours? What's here!

11.play: 'Against the Duke of Suffolk, for enclosing the

12.play: commons of Melford.' How now, sir knave!

13.play: PETER: [Giving his petition]  Against my master, Thomas

14.play: Horner, for saying that the Duke of York was rightful

15.play: heir to the crown.

16.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What sayst thou? did the Duke of York say he was

17.play: rightful heir to the crown?

18.play: PETER: That my master was? no, forsooth: my master said

19.play: that he was, and that the king was an usurper.

20.play: SUFFOLK: Who is there?

21.play: Take this fellow in, and send for

22.play: his master with a pursuivant presently: we'll hear

23.play: more of your matter before the King.

24.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And as for you, that love to be protected

25.play: Under the wings of our protector's grace,

26.play: Begin your suits anew, and sue to him.

27.play: Away, base cullions! Suffolk, let them go.

28.play: ALL: Come, let's be gone.

29.play: QUEEN MARGARET: My Lord of Suffolk, say, is this the guise,

30.play: Is this the fashion in the court of England?

31.play: Is this the government of Britain's isle,

32.play: And this the royalty of Albion's king?

33.play: What shall King Henry be a pupil still

34.play: Under the surly Gloucester's governance?

35.play: Am I a queen in title and in style,

36.play: And must be made a subject to a duke?

37.play: I tell thee, Pole, when in the city Tours

38.play: Thou ran'st a tilt in honour of my love

39.play: And stolest away the ladies' hearts of France,

40.play: I thought King Henry had resembled thee

42.play: But all his mind is bent to holiness,

43.play: To number Ave-Maries on his beads;

44.play: His champions are the prophets and apostles,

45.play: His weapons holy saws of sacred writ,

46.play: His study is his tilt-yard, and his loves

47.play: Are brazen images of canonized saints.

48.play: I would the college of the cardinals

49.play: Would choose him pope, and carry him to Rome,

50.play: And set the triple crown upon his head:

51.play: That were a state fit for his holiness.

52.play: SUFFOLK: Madam, be patient: as I was cause

53.play: Your highness came to England, so will I

54.play: In England work your grace's full content.

55.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Beside the haughty protector, have we Beaufort,

57.play: And grumbling York: and not the least of these

58.play: But can do more in England than the king.

59.play: SUFFOLK: And he of these that can do most of all

60.play: Cannot do more in England than the Nevils:

61.play: Salisbury and Warwick are no simple peers.

62.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Not all these lords do vex me half so much

63.play: As that proud dame, the lord protector's wife.

64.play: She sweeps it through the court with troops of ladies,

65.play: More like an empress than Duke Humphrey's wife:

66.play: Strangers in court do take her for the queen:

67.play: She bears a duke's revenues on her back,

68.play: And in her heart she scorns our poverty:

69.play: Shall I not live to be avenged on her?

70.play: Contemptuous base-born callet as she is,

71.play: She vaunted 'mongst her minions t'other day,

72.play: The very train of her worst wearing gown

73.play: Was better worth than all my father's lands,

74.play: Till Suffolk gave two dukedoms for his daughter.

75.play: SUFFOLK: Madam, myself have limed a bush for her,

76.play: And placed a quire of such enticing birds,

77.play: That she will light to listen to the lays,

78.play: And never mount to trouble you again.

79.play: So, let her rest: and, madam, list to me;

80.play: For I am bold to counsel you in this.

81.play: Although we fancy not the cardinal,

82.play: Yet must we join with him and with the lords,

83.play: Till we have brought Duke Humphrey in disgrace.

84.play: As for the Duke of York, this late complaint

85.play: Will make but little for his benefit.

86.play: So, one by one, we'll weed them all at last,

1.play: KING HENRY VI: Stand forth, Dame Eleanor Cobham, Gloucester's wife:

2.play: In sight of God and us, your guilt is great:

3.play: Receive the sentence of the law for sins

4.play: Such as by God's book are adjudged to death.

5.play: You four, from hence to prison back again;

6.play: From thence unto the place of execution:

7.play: The witch in Smithfield shall be burn'd to ashes,

8.play: And you three shall be strangled on the gallows.

9.play: You, madam, for you are more nobly born,

10.play: Despoiled of your honour in your life,

11.play: Shall, after three days' open penance done,

12.play: Live in your country here in banishment,

13.play: With Sir John Stanley, in the Isle of Man.

14.play: DUCHESS: Welcome is banishment; welcome were my death.

15.play: GLOUCESTER: Eleanor, the law, thou see'st, hath judged thee:

16.play: I cannot justify whom the law condemns.

17.play: Mine eyes are full of tears, my heart of grief.

18.play: Ah, Humphrey, this dishonour in thine age

19.play: Will bring thy head with sorrow to the ground!

20.play: I beseech your majesty, give me leave to go;

21.play: Sorrow would solace and mine age would ease.

22.play: KING HENRY VI: Stay, Humphrey Duke of Gloucester: ere thou go,

23.play: Give up thy staff: Henry will to himself

24.play: Protector be; and God shall be my hope,

25.play: My stay, my guide and lantern to my feet:

26.play: And go in peace, Humphrey, no less beloved

27.play: Than when thou wert protector to thy King.

28.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I see no reason why a king of years

29.play: Should be to be protected like a child.

30.play: God and King Henry govern England's realm.

31.play: Give up your staff, sir, and the king his realm.

32.play: GLOUCESTER: My staff? here, noble Henry, is my staff:

33.play: As willingly do I the same resign

34.play: As e'er thy father Henry made it mine;

35.play: And even as willingly at thy feet I leave it

36.play: As others would ambitiously receive it.

37.play: Farewell, good king: when I am dead and gone,

38.play: May honourable peace attend thy throne!

39.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Why, now is Henry king, and Margaret queen;

40.play: And Humphrey Duke of Gloucester scarce himself,

41.play: That bears so shrewd a maim; two pulls at once;

42.play: His lady banish'd, and a limb lopp'd off.

43.play: This staff of honour raught, there let it stand

44.play: Where it best fits to be, in Henry's hand.

45.play: SUFFOLK: Thus droops this lofty pine and hangs his sprays;

46.play: Thus Eleanor's pride dies in her youngest days.

47.play: YORK: Lords, let him go. Please it your majesty,

48.play: This is the day appointed for the combat;

49.play: And ready are the appellant and defendant,

50.play: The armourer and his man, to enter the lists,

51.play: So please your highness to behold the fight.

52.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ay, good my lord; for purposely therefore

53.play: Left I the court, to see this quarrel tried.

54.play: KING HENRY VI: O God's name, see the lists and all things fit:

55.play: Here let them end it; and God defend the right!

56.play: YORK: I never saw a fellow worse bested,

57.play: Or more afraid to fight, than is the appellant,

58.play: The servant of this armourer, my lords.

59.play: HORNER: Let it come, i' faith, and I'll pledge you all; and

61.play: First 'Prentice	Here, Peter, I drink to thee: and be not afraid.

62.play: Second 'Prentice	Be merry, Peter, and fear not thy master: fight

64.play: PETER: I thank you all: drink, and pray for me, I pray

65.play: you; for I think I have taken my last draught in

66.play: this world. Here, Robin, an if I die, I give thee

67.play: my apron: and, Will, thou shalt have my hammer:

68.play: and here, Tom, take all the money that I have. O

69.play: Lord bless me! I pray God! for I am never able to

70.play: deal with my master, he hath learnt me so much fence already.

71.play: SALISBURY: Come, leave your drinking, and fall to blows.

76.play: SALISBURY: Thump! then see thou thump thy master well.

77.play: HORNER: Masters, I am come hither, as it were, upon my man's

78.play: instigation, to prove him a knave and myself an

79.play: honest man: and touching the Duke of York, I will

80.play: take my death, I never meant him any ill, nor the

81.play: king, nor the queen: and therefore, Peter, have at

83.play: YORK: Dispatch: this knave's tongue begins to double.

84.play: Sound, trumpets, alarum to the combatants!

85.play: HORNER: Hold, Peter, hold! I confess, I confess treason.

86.play: YORK: Take away his weapon. Fellow, thank God, and the

87.play: good wine in thy master's way.

88.play: PETER: O God, have I overcome mine enemy in this presence?

89.play: O Peter, thou hast prevailed in right!

90.play: KING HENRY VI: Go, take hence that traitor from our sight;

91.play: For his death we do perceive his guilt:

92.play: And God in justice hath revealed to us

93.play: The truth and innocence of this poor fellow,

94.play: Which he had thought to have murder'd wrongfully.

95.play: Come, fellow, follow us for thy reward.

1.play: [Enter GLOUCESTER and his Servingmen, in mourning cloaks]

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Thus sometimes hath the brightest day a cloud;

3.play: And after summer evermore succeeds

4.play: Barren winter, with his wrathful nipping cold:

5.play: So cares and joys abound, as seasons fleet.

6.play: Sirs, what's o'clock?

7.play: Ten is the hour that was appointed me

8.play: To watch the coming of my punish'd duchess:

9.play: Uneath may she endure the flinty streets,

10.play: To tread them with her tender-feeling feet.

11.play: Sweet Nell, ill can thy noble mind abrook

12.play: The abject people gazing on thy face,

13.play: With envious looks, laughing at thy shame,

14.play: That erst did follow thy proud chariot-wheels

15.play: When thou didst ride in triumph through the streets.

16.play: But, soft! I think she comes; and I'll prepare

17.play: My tear-stain'd eyes to see her miseries.

18.play: No, stir not, for your lives; let her pass by.

19.play: DUCHESS: Come you, my lord, to see my open shame?

20.play: Now thou dost penance too. Look how they gaze!

21.play: See how the giddy multitude do point,

22.play: And nod their heads, and throw their eyes on thee!

23.play: Ah, Gloucester, hide thee from their hateful looks,

24.play: And, in thy closet pent up, rue my shame,

25.play: And ban thine enemies, both mine and thine!

26.play: GLOUCESTER: Be patient, gentle Nell; forget this grief.

27.play: DUCHESS: Ah, Gloucester, teach me to forget myself!

28.play: For whilst I think I am thy married wife

29.play: And thou a prince, protector of this land,

30.play: Methinks I should not thus be led along,

31.play: Mail'd up in shame, with papers on my back,

32.play: And followed with a rabble that rejoice

33.play: To see my tears and hear my deep-fet groans.

34.play: The ruthless flint doth cut my tender feet,

35.play: And when I start, the envious people laugh

36.play: And bid me be advised how I tread.

37.play: Ah, Humphrey, can I bear this shameful yoke?

38.play: Trow'st thou that e'er I'll look upon the world,

39.play: Or count them happy that enjoy the sun?

40.play: No; dark shall be my light and night my day;

41.play: To think upon my pomp shall be my hell.

42.play: Sometime I'll say, I am Duke Humphrey's wife,

43.play: And he a prince and ruler of the land:

44.play: Yet so he ruled and such a prince he was

45.play: As he stood by whilst I, his forlorn duchess,

46.play: Was made a wonder and a pointing-stock

48.play: But be thou mild and blush not at my shame,

49.play: Nor stir at nothing till the axe of death

50.play: Hang over thee, as, sure, it shortly will;

51.play: For Suffolk, he that can do all in all

52.play: With her that hateth thee and hates us all,

53.play: And York and impious Beaufort, that false priest,

54.play: Have all limed bushes to betray thy wings,

55.play: And, fly thou how thou canst, they'll tangle thee:

56.play: But fear not thou, until thy foot be snared,

57.play: Nor never seek prevention of thy foes.

58.play: GLOUCESTER: Ah, Nell, forbear! thou aimest all awry;

59.play: I must offend before I be attainted;

60.play: And had I twenty times so many foes,

61.play: And each of them had twenty times their power,

62.play: All these could not procure me any scathe,

63.play: So long as I am loyal, true and crimeless.

64.play: Wouldst have me rescue thee from this reproach?

65.play: Why, yet thy scandal were not wiped away

66.play: But I in danger for the breach of law.

67.play: Thy greatest help is quiet, gentle Nell:

68.play: I pray thee, sort thy heart to patience;

69.play: These few days' wonder will be quickly worn.

70.play: And my consent ne'er ask'd herein before!

71.play: This is close dealing. Well, I will be there.

72.play: My Nell, I take my leave: and, master sheriff,

73.play: Let not her penance exceed the king's commission.

74.play: Must you, Sir John, protect my lady here?

75.play: STANLEY: So am I given in charge, may't please your grace.

76.play: GLOUCESTER: Entreat her not the worse in that I pray

77.play: You use her well: the world may laugh again;

78.play: And I may live to do you kindness if

79.play: You do it her: and so, Sir John, farewell!

80.play: DUCHESS: What, gone, my lord, and bid me not farewell!

81.play: GLOUCESTER: Witness my tears, I cannot stay to speak.

82.play: DUCHESS: Art thou gone too? all comfort go with thee!

83.play: For none abides with me: my joy is death;

84.play: Death, at whose name I oft have been afear'd,

85.play: Because I wish'd this world's eternity.

86.play: Stanley, I prithee, go, and take me hence;

87.play: I care not whither, for I beg no favour,

88.play: Only convey me where thou art commanded.

89.play: STANLEY: Why, madam, that is to the Isle of Man;

90.play: There to be used according to your state.

91.play: DUCHESS: That's bad enough, for I am but reproach:

92.play: And shall I then be used reproachfully?

93.play: STANLEY: Like to a duchess, and Duke Humphrey's lady;

94.play: According to that state you shall be used.

95.play: DUCHESS: Sheriff, farewell, and better than I fare,

96.play: Although thou hast been conduct of my shame.

97.play: Ay, ay, farewell; thy office is discharged.

99.play: STANLEY: Madam, your penance done, throw off this sheet,

100.play: And go we to attire you for our journey.

101.play: DUCHESS: My shame will not be shifted with my sheet:

102.play: No, it will hang upon my richest robes

103.play: And show itself, attire me how I can.

104.play: Go, lead the way; I long to see my prison.

1.play: KING HENRY VI: I muse my Lord of Gloucester is not come:

2.play: 'Tis not his wont to be the hindmost man,

3.play: Whate'er occasion keeps him from us now.

4.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Can you not see? or will ye not observe

5.play: The strangeness of his alter'd countenance?

6.play: With what a majesty he bears himself,

7.play: How insolent of late he is become,

8.play: How proud, how peremptory, and unlike himself?

9.play: We know the time since he was mild and affable,

10.play: And if we did but glance a far-off look,

11.play: Immediately he was upon his knee,

12.play: That all the court admired him for submission:

13.play: But meet him now, and, be it in the morn,

14.play: When every one will give the time of day,

15.play: He knits his brow and shows an angry eye,

16.play: And passeth by with stiff unbowed knee,

17.play: Disdaining duty that to us belongs.

18.play: Small curs are not regarded when they grin;

19.play: But great men tremble when the lion roars;

20.play: And Humphrey is no little man in England.

21.play: First note that he is near you in descent,

22.play: And should you fall, he as the next will mount.

23.play: Me seemeth then it is no policy,

24.play: Respecting what a rancorous mind he bears

25.play: And his advantage following your decease,

26.play: That he should come about your royal person

27.play: Or be admitted to your highness' council.

28.play: By flattery hath he won the commons' hearts,

29.play: And when he please to make commotion,

30.play: 'Tis to be fear'd they all will follow him.

31.play: Now 'tis the spring, and weeds are shallow-rooted;

32.play: Suffer them now, and they'll o'ergrow the garden

33.play: And choke the herbs for want of husbandry.

34.play: The reverent care I bear unto my lord

35.play: Made me collect these dangers in the duke.

36.play: If it be fond, call it a woman's fear;

37.play: Which fear if better reasons can supplant,

38.play: I will subscribe and say I wrong'd the duke.

39.play: My Lord of Suffolk, Buckingham, and York,

40.play: Reprove my allegation, if you can;

41.play: Or else conclude my words effectual.

42.play: SUFFOLK: Well hath your highness seen into this duke;

43.play: And, had I first been put to speak my mind,

44.play: I think I should have told your grace's tale.

46.play: Upon my life, began her devilish practises:

47.play: Or, if he were not privy to those faults,

48.play: Yet, by reputing of his high descent,

49.play: As next the king he was successive heir,

50.play: And such high vaunts of his nobility,

51.play: Did instigate the bedlam brain-sick duchess

52.play: By wicked means to frame our sovereign's fall.

53.play: Smooth runs the water where the brook is deep;

54.play: And in his simple show he harbours treason.

55.play: The fox barks not when he would steal the lamb.

56.play: No, no, my sovereign; Gloucester is a man

57.play: Unsounded yet and full of deep deceit.

58.play: CARDINAL: Did he not, contrary to form of law,

59.play: Devise strange deaths for small offences done?

60.play: YORK: And did he not, in his protectorship,

61.play: Levy great sums of money through the realm

62.play: For soldiers' pay in France, and never sent it?

63.play: By means whereof the towns each day revolted.

64.play: BUCKINGHAM: Tut, these are petty faults to faults unknown.

65.play: Which time will bring to light in smooth

67.play: KING HENRY VI: My lords, at once: the care you have of us,

68.play: To mow down thorns that would annoy our foot,

69.play: Is worthy praise: but, shall I speak my conscience,

70.play: Our kinsman Gloucester is as innocent

71.play: From meaning treason to our royal person

72.play: As is the sucking lamb or harmless dove:

73.play: The duke is virtuous, mild and too well given

74.play: To dream on evil or to work my downfall.

75.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ah, what's more dangerous than this fond affiance!

76.play: Seems he a dove? his feathers are but borrowed,

77.play: For he's disposed as the hateful raven:

78.play: Is he a lamb? his skin is surely lent him,

79.play: For he's inclined as is the ravenous wolf.

80.play: Who cannot steal a shape that means deceit?

81.play: Take heed, my lord; the welfare of us all

82.play: Hangs on the cutting short that fraudful man.

83.play: SOMERSET: All health unto my gracious sovereign!

84.play: KING HENRY VI: Welcome, Lord Somerset. What news from France?

85.play: SOMERSET: That all your interest in those territories

86.play: Is utterly bereft you; all is lost.

87.play: KING HENRY VI: Cold news, Lord Somerset: but God's will be done!

88.play: YORK: [Aside]  Cold news for me; for I had hope of France

89.play: As firmly as I hope for fertile England.

90.play: Thus are my blossoms blasted in the bud

91.play: And caterpillars eat my leaves away;

92.play: But I will remedy this gear ere long,

93.play: Or sell my title for a glorious grave.

94.play: GLOUCESTER: All happiness unto my lord the king!

95.play: Pardon, my liege, that I have stay'd so long.

96.play: SUFFOLK: Nay, Gloucester, know that thou art come too soon,

97.play: Unless thou wert more loyal than thou art:

98.play: I do arrest thee of high treason here.

99.play: GLOUCESTER: Well, Suffolk, thou shalt not see me blush

100.play: Nor change my countenance for this arrest:

101.play: A heart unspotted is not easily daunted.

102.play: The purest spring is not so free from mud

103.play: As I am clear from treason to my sovereign:

104.play: Who can accuse me? wherein am I guilty?

105.play: YORK: 'Tis thought, my lord, that you took bribes of France,

106.play: And, being protector, stayed the soldiers' pay;

107.play: By means whereof his highness hath lost France.

108.play: GLOUCESTER: Is it but thought so? what are they that think it?

109.play: I never robb'd the soldiers of their pay,

110.play: Nor ever had one penny bribe from France.

111.play: So help me God, as I have watch'd the night,

112.play: Ay, night by night, in studying good for England,

113.play: That doit that e'er I wrested from the king,

114.play: Or any groat I hoarded to my use,

115.play: Be brought against me at my trial-day!

116.play: No; many a pound of mine own proper store,

117.play: Because I would not tax the needy commons,

118.play: Have I disbursed to the garrisons,

120.play: CARDINAL: It serves you well, my lord, to say so much.

121.play: GLOUCESTER: I say no more than truth, so help me God!

122.play: YORK: In your protectorship you did devise

123.play: Strange tortures for offenders never heard of,

124.play: That England was defamed by tyranny.

125.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, 'tis well known that, whiles I was

127.play: Pity was all the fault that was in me;

128.play: For I should melt at an offender's tears,

129.play: And lowly words were ransom for their fault.

130.play: Unless it were a bloody murderer,

131.play: Or foul felonious thief that fleeced poor passengers,

132.play: I never gave them condign punishment:

133.play: Murder indeed, that bloody sin, I tortured

134.play: Above the felon or what trespass else.

135.play: SUFFOLK: My lord, these faults are easy, quickly answered:

136.play: But mightier crimes are laid unto your charge,

137.play: Whereof you cannot easily purge yourself.

138.play: I do arrest you in his highness' name;

139.play: And here commit you to my lord cardinal

140.play: To keep, until your further time of trial.

141.play: KING HENRY VI: My lord of Gloucester, 'tis my special hope

142.play: That you will clear yourself from all suspect:

143.play: My conscience tells me you are innocent.

144.play: GLOUCESTER: Ah, gracious lord, these days are dangerous:

145.play: Virtue is choked with foul ambition

146.play: And charity chased hence by rancour's hand;

148.play: And equity exiled your highness' land.

149.play: I know their complot is to have my life,

150.play: And if my death might make this island happy,

151.play: And prove the period of their tyranny,

152.play: I would expend it with all willingness:

153.play: But mine is made the prologue to their play;

154.play: For thousands more, that yet suspect no peril,

155.play: Will not conclude their plotted tragedy.

156.play: Beaufort's red sparkling eyes blab his heart's malice,

157.play: And Suffolk's cloudy brow his stormy hate;

158.play: Sharp Buckingham unburthens with his tongue

159.play: The envious load that lies upon his heart;

160.play: And dogged York, that reaches at the moon,

161.play: Whose overweening arm I have pluck'd back,

162.play: By false accuse doth level at my life:

163.play: And you, my sovereign lady, with the rest,

164.play: Causeless have laid disgraces on my head,

165.play: And with your best endeavour have stirr'd up

166.play: My liefest liege to be mine enemy:

167.play: Ay, all you have laid your heads together--

168.play: Myself had notice of your conventicles--

169.play: And all to make away my guiltless life.

170.play: I shall not want false witness to condemn me,

171.play: Nor store of treasons to augment my guilt;

172.play: The ancient proverb will be well effected:

173.play: 'A staff is quickly found to beat a dog.'

174.play: CARDINAL: My liege, his railing is intolerable:

175.play: If those that care to keep your royal person

176.play: From treason's secret knife and traitors' rage

177.play: Be thus upbraided, chid and rated at,

178.play: And the offender granted scope of speech,

179.play: 'Twill make them cool in zeal unto your grace.

180.play: SUFFOLK: Hath he not twit our sovereign lady here

181.play: With ignominious words, though clerkly couch'd,

182.play: As if she had suborned some to swear

183.play: False allegations to o'erthrow his state?

184.play: QUEEN MARGARET: But I can give the loser leave to chide.

185.play: GLOUCESTER: Far truer spoke than meant: I lose, indeed;

186.play: Beshrew the winners, for they play'd me false!

187.play: And well such losers may have leave to speak.

188.play: BUCKINGHAM: He'll wrest the sense and hold us here all day:

189.play: Lord cardinal, he is your prisoner.

190.play: CARDINAL: Sirs, take away the duke, and guard him sure.

191.play: GLOUCESTER: Ah! thus King Henry throws away his crutch

192.play: Before his legs be firm to bear his body.

193.play: Thus is the shepherd beaten from thy side,

194.play: And wolves are gnarling who shall gnaw thee first.

195.play: Ah, that my fear were false! ah, that it were!

196.play: For, good King Henry, thy decay I fear.

197.play: KING HENRY VI: My lords, what to your wisdoms seemeth best,

198.play: Do or undo, as if ourself were here.

199.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What, will your highness leave the parliament?

200.play: KING HENRY VI: Ay, Margaret; my heart is drown'd with grief,

201.play: Whose flood begins to flow within mine eyes,

202.play: My body round engirt with misery,

203.play: For what's more miserable than discontent?

204.play: Ah, uncle Humphrey! in thy face I see

205.play: The map of honour, truth and loyalty:

206.play: And yet, good Humphrey, is the hour to come

207.play: That e'er I proved thee false or fear'd thy faith.

208.play: What louring star now envies thy estate,

209.play: That these great lords and Margaret our queen

210.play: Do seek subversion of thy harmless life?

211.play: Thou never didst them wrong, nor no man wrong;

212.play: And as the butcher takes away the calf

213.play: And binds the wretch, and beats it when it strays,

214.play: Bearing it to the bloody slaughter-house,

215.play: Even so remorseless have they borne him hence;

216.play: And as the dam runs lowing up and down,

217.play: Looking the way her harmless young one went,

218.play: And can do nought but wail her darling's loss,

219.play: Even so myself bewails good Gloucester's case

220.play: With sad unhelpful tears, and with dimm'd eyes

221.play: Look after him and cannot do him good,

222.play: So mighty are his vowed enemies.

223.play: His fortunes I will weep; and, 'twixt each groan

224.play: Say 'Who's a traitor? Gloucester he is none.'

225.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Free lords, cold snow melts with the sun's hot beams.

226.play: Henry my lord is cold in great affairs,

227.play: Too full of foolish pity, and Gloucester's show

228.play: Beguiles him as the mournful crocodile

230.play: Or as the snake roll'd in a flowering bank,

231.play: With shining chequer'd slough, doth sting a child

232.play: That for the beauty thinks it excellent.

233.play: Believe me, lords, were none more wise than I--

234.play: And yet herein I judge mine own wit good--

235.play: This Gloucester should be quickly rid the world,

236.play: To rid us of the fear we have of him.

237.play: CARDINAL: That he should die is worthy policy;

238.play: But yet we want a colour for his death:

239.play: 'Tis meet he be condemn'd by course of law.

240.play: SUFFOLK: But, in my mind, that were no policy:

241.play: The king will labour still to save his life,

242.play: The commons haply rise, to save his life;

243.play: And yet we have but trivial argument,

244.play: More than mistrust, that shows him worthy death.

245.play: YORK: So that, by this, you would not have him die.

246.play: SUFFOLK: Ah, York, no man alive so fain as I!

247.play: YORK: 'Tis York that hath more reason for his death.

248.play: But, my lord cardinal, and you, my Lord of Suffolk,

249.play: Say as you think, and speak it from your souls,

250.play: Were't not all one, an empty eagle were set

251.play: To guard the chicken from a hungry kite,

252.play: As place Duke Humphrey for the king's protector?

253.play: QUEEN MARGARET: So the poor chicken should be sure of death.

254.play: SUFFOLK: Madam, 'tis true; and were't not madness, then,

255.play: To make the fox surveyor of the fold?

256.play: Who being accused a crafty murderer,

257.play: His guilt should be but idly posted over,

258.play: Because his purpose is not executed.

259.play: No; let him die, in that he is a fox,

260.play: By nature proved an enemy to the flock,

261.play: Before his chaps be stain'd with crimson blood,

262.play: As Humphrey, proved by reasons, to my liege.

263.play: And do not stand on quillets how to slay him:

264.play: Be it by gins, by snares, by subtlety,

265.play: Sleeping or waking, 'tis no matter how,

266.play: So he be dead; for that is good deceit

267.play: Which mates him first that first intends deceit.

268.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thrice-noble Suffolk, 'tis resolutely spoke.

269.play: SUFFOLK: Not resolute, except so much were done;

270.play: For things are often spoke and seldom meant:

271.play: But that my heart accordeth with my tongue,

273.play: And to preserve my sovereign from his foe,

274.play: Say but the word, and I will be his priest.

275.play: CARDINAL: But I would have him dead, my Lord of Suffolk,

276.play: Ere you can take due orders for a priest:

277.play: Say you consent and censure well the deed,

279.play: I tender so the safety of my liege.

280.play: SUFFOLK: Here is my hand, the deed is worthy doing.

281.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And so say I.

282.play: YORK: And I    and now we three have spoke it,

283.play: It skills not greatly who impugns our doom.

284.play: CARDINAL: A breach that craves a quick expedient stop!

285.play: What counsel give you in this weighty cause?

286.play: YORK: That Somerset be sent as regent thither:

287.play: 'Tis meet that lucky ruler be employ'd;

288.play: Witness the fortune he hath had in France.

289.play: SOMERSET: If York, with all his far-fet policy,

290.play: Had been the regent there instead of me,

291.play: He never would have stay'd in France so long.

292.play: YORK: No, not to lose it all, as thou hast done:

293.play: I rather would have lost my life betimes

294.play: Than bring a burthen of dishonour home

295.play: By staying there so long till all were lost.

296.play: Show me one scar character'd on thy skin:

297.play: Men's flesh preserved so whole do seldom win.

298.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Nay, then, this spark will prove a raging fire,

299.play: If wind and fuel be brought to feed it with:

300.play: No more, good York; sweet Somerset, be still:

301.play: Thy fortune, York, hadst thou been regent there,

302.play: Might happily have proved far worse than his.

303.play: YORK: What, worse than nought? nay, then, a shame take all!

304.play: SOMERSET: And, in the number, thee that wishest shame!

305.play: CARDINAL: My Lord of York, try what your fortune is.

306.play: The uncivil kerns of Ireland are in arms

307.play: And temper clay with blood of Englishmen:

308.play: To Ireland will you lead a band of men,

309.play: Collected choicely, from each county some,

310.play: And try your hap against the Irishmen?

311.play: YORK: I will, my lord, so please his majesty.

312.play: SUFFOLK: Why, our authority is his consent,

313.play: And what we do establish he confirms:

314.play: Then, noble York, take thou this task in hand.

315.play: YORK: I am content: provide me soldiers, lords,

316.play: Whiles I take order for mine own affairs.

317.play: SUFFOLK: A charge, Lord York, that I will see perform'd.

318.play: But now return we to the false Duke Humphrey.

319.play: CARDINAL: No more of him; for I will deal with him

320.play: That henceforth he shall trouble us no more.

321.play: And so break off; the day is almost spent:

322.play: Lord Suffolk, you and I must talk of that event.

323.play: YORK: My Lord of Suffolk, within fourteen days

324.play: At Bristol I expect my soldiers;

325.play: For there I'll ship them all for Ireland.

326.play: SUFFOLK: I'll see it truly done, my Lord of York.

327.play: YORK: Now, York, or never, steel thy fearful thoughts,

329.play: Be that thou hopest to be, or what thou art

330.play: Resign to death; it is not worth the enjoying:

331.play: Let pale-faced fear keep with the mean-born man,

332.play: And find no harbour in a royal heart.

333.play: Faster than spring-time showers comes thought

335.play: And not a thought but thinks on dignity.

336.play: My brain more busy than the labouring spider

337.play: Weaves tedious snares to trap mine enemies.

339.play: To send me packing with an host of men:

340.play: I fear me you but warm the starved snake,

341.play: Who, cherish'd in your breasts, will sting

343.play: 'Twas men I lack'd and you will give them me:

344.play: I take it kindly; and yet be well assured

345.play: You put sharp weapons in a madman's hands.

346.play: Whiles I in Ireland nourish a mighty band,

347.play: I will stir up in England some black storm

348.play: Shall blow ten thousand souls to heaven or hell;

349.play: And this fell tempest shall not cease to rage

350.play: Until the golden circuit on my head,

351.play: Like to the glorious sun's transparent beams,

352.play: Do calm the fury of this mad-bred flaw.

353.play: And, for a minister of my intent,

354.play: I have seduced a headstrong Kentishman,

356.play: To make commotion, as full well he can,

357.play: Under the title of John Mortimer.

358.play: In Ireland have I seen this stubborn Cade

359.play: Oppose himself against a troop of kerns,

360.play: And fought so long, till that his thighs with darts

361.play: Were almost like a sharp-quill'd porpentine;

362.play: And, in the end being rescued, I have seen

363.play: Him caper upright like a wild Morisco,

364.play: Shaking the bloody darts as he his bells.

365.play: Full often, like a shag-hair'd crafty kern,

366.play: Hath he conversed with the enemy,

367.play: And undiscover'd come to me again

368.play: And given me notice of their villanies.

369.play: This devil here shall be my substitute;

370.play: For that John Mortimer, which now is dead,

371.play: In face, in gait, in speech, he doth resemble:

372.play: By this I shall perceive the commons' mind,

373.play: How they affect the house and claim of York.

374.play: Say he be taken, rack'd and tortured,

375.play: I know no pain they can inflict upon him

376.play: Will make him say I moved him to those arms.

377.play: Say that he thrive, as 'tis great like he will,

378.play: Why, then from Ireland come I with my strength

379.play: And reap the harvest which that rascal sow'd;

380.play: For Humphrey being dead, as he shall be,

381.play: And Henry put apart, the next for me.

1.play: [Enter certain Murderers, hastily]

2.play: [Enter SUFFOLK]

3.play: SUFFOLK: Now, sirs, have you dispatch'd this thing?

4.play: Why, that's well said. Go, get you to my house;

5.play: I will reward you for this venturous deed.

6.play: The king and all the peers are here at hand.

7.play: Have you laid fair the bed? Is all things well,

8.play: According as I gave directions?

9.play: Away! be gone.

10.play: KING HENRY VI: Go, call our uncle to our presence straight;

11.play: Say we intend to try his grace to-day.

12.play: If he be guilty, as 'tis published.

13.play: SUFFOLK: I'll call him presently, my noble lord.

14.play: KING HENRY VI: Lords, take your places; and, I pray you all,

15.play: Proceed no straiter 'gainst our uncle Gloucester

16.play: Than from true evidence of good esteem

17.play: He be approved in practise culpable.

18.play: QUEEN MARGARET: God forbid any malice should prevail,

19.play: That faultless may condemn a nobleman!

20.play: Pray God he may acquit him of suspicion!

21.play: KING HENRY VI: I thank thee, Meg; these words content me much.

22.play: How now! why look'st thou pale? why tremblest thou?

23.play: Where is our uncle? what's the matter, Suffolk?

24.play: SUFFOLK: Dead in his bed, my lord; Gloucester is dead.

25.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Marry, God forfend!

26.play: CARDINAL: God's secret judgment: I did dream to-night

27.play: The duke was dumb and could not speak a word.

28.play: QUEEN MARGARET: How fares my lord? Help, lords! the king is dead.

29.play: SOMERSET: Rear up his body; wring him by the nose.

30.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Run, go, help, help! O Henry, ope thine eyes!

31.play: SUFFOLK: He doth revive again: madam, be patient.

32.play: KING HENRY VI: O heavenly God!

33.play: QUEEN MARGARET: How fares my gracious lord?

34.play: SUFFOLK: Comfort, my sovereign! gracious Henry, comfort!

35.play: KING HENRY VI: What, doth my Lord of Suffolk comfort me?

36.play: Came he right now to sing a raven's note,

37.play: Whose dismal tune bereft my vital powers;

38.play: And thinks he that the chirping of a wren,

39.play: By crying comfort from a hollow breast,

40.play: Can chase away the first-conceived sound?

41.play: Hide not thy poison with such sugar'd words;

42.play: Lay not thy hands on me; forbear, I say;

43.play: Their touch affrights me as a serpent's sting.

44.play: Thou baleful messenger, out of my sight!

46.play: Sits in grim majesty, to fright the world.

47.play: Look not upon me, for thine eyes are wounding:

48.play: Yet do not go away: come, basilisk,

49.play: And kill the innocent gazer with thy sight;

50.play: For in the shade of death I shall find joy;

51.play: In life but double death, now Gloucester's dead.

52.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Why do you rate my Lord of Suffolk thus?

53.play: Although the duke was enemy to him,

54.play: Yet he most Christian-like laments his death:

55.play: And for myself, foe as he was to me,

56.play: Might liquid tears or heart-offending groans

57.play: Or blood-consuming sighs recall his life,

58.play: I would be blind with weeping, sick with groans,

59.play: Look pale as primrose with blood-drinking sighs,

60.play: And all to have the noble duke alive.

61.play: What know I how the world may deem of me?

62.play: For it is known we were but hollow friends:

63.play: It may be judged I made the duke away;

64.play: So shall my name with slander's tongue be wounded,

65.play: And princes' courts be fill'd with my reproach.

66.play: This get I by his death: ay me, unhappy!

67.play: To be a queen, and crown'd with infamy!

68.play: KING HENRY VI: Ah, woe is me for Gloucester, wretched man!

69.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Be woe for me, more wretched than he is.

70.play: What, dost thou turn away and hide thy face?

71.play: I am no loathsome leper; look on me.

72.play: What! art thou, like the adder, waxen deaf?

73.play: Be poisonous too and kill thy forlorn queen.

74.play: Is all thy comfort shut in Gloucester's tomb?

75.play: Why, then, dame Margaret was ne'er thy joy.

76.play: Erect his statue and worship it,

77.play: And make my image but an alehouse sign.

78.play: Was I for this nigh wreck'd upon the sea

79.play: And twice by awkward wind from England's bank

80.play: Drove back again unto my native clime?

81.play: What boded this, but well forewarning wind

82.play: Did seem to say 'Seek not a scorpion's nest,

83.play: Nor set no footing on this unkind shore'?

84.play: What did I then, but cursed the gentle gusts

85.play: And he that loosed them forth their brazen caves:

86.play: And bid them blow towards England's blessed shore,

87.play: Or turn our stern upon a dreadful rock

88.play: Yet AEolus would not be a murderer,

89.play: But left that hateful office unto thee:

90.play: The pretty-vaulting sea refused to drown me,

91.play: Knowing that thou wouldst have me drown'd on shore,

92.play: With tears as salt as sea, through thy unkindness:

93.play: The splitting rocks cower'd in the sinking sands

94.play: And would not dash me with their ragged sides,

95.play: Because thy flinty heart, more hard than they,

96.play: Might in thy palace perish Margaret.

97.play: As far as I could ken thy chalky cliffs,

98.play: When from thy shore the tempest beat us back,

99.play: I stood upon the hatches in the storm,

100.play: And when the dusky sky began to rob

101.play: My earnest-gaping sight of thy land's view,

102.play: I took a costly jewel from my neck,

103.play: A heart it was, bound in with diamonds,

104.play: And threw it towards thy land: the sea received it,

105.play: And so I wish'd thy body might my heart:

106.play: And even with this I lost fair England's view

107.play: And bid mine eyes be packing with my heart

108.play: And call'd them blind and dusky spectacles,

109.play: For losing ken of Albion's wished coast.

110.play: How often have I tempted Suffolk's tongue,

111.play: The agent of thy foul inconstancy,

112.play: To sit and witch me, as Ascanius did

113.play: When he to madding Dido would unfold

114.play: His father's acts commenced in burning Troy!

115.play: Am I not witch'd like her? or thou not false like him?

116.play: Ay me, I can no more! die, Margaret!

117.play: For Henry weeps that thou dost live so long.

118.play: WARWICK: It is reported, mighty sovereign,

119.play: That good Duke Humphrey traitorously is murder'd

120.play: By Suffolk and the Cardinal Beaufort's means.

121.play: The commons, like an angry hive of bees

122.play: That want their leader, scatter up and down

123.play: And care not who they sting in his revenge.

124.play: Myself have calm'd their spleenful mutiny,

125.play: Until they hear the order of his death.

126.play: KING HENRY VI: That he is dead, good Warwick, 'tis too true;

127.play: But how he died God knows, not Henry:

128.play: [Enter his chamber, view his breathless corpse, And comment then upon his sudden death.]

130.play: WARWICK: That shall I do, my liege. Stay, Salisbury,

131.play: With the rude multitude till I return.

132.play: KING HENRY VI: O Thou that judgest all things, stay my thoughts,

133.play: My thoughts, that labour to persuade my soul

134.play: Some violent hands were laid on Humphrey's life!

135.play: If my suspect be false, forgive me, God,

136.play: For judgment only doth belong to thee.

137.play: Fain would I go to chafe his paly lips

138.play: With twenty thousand kisses, and to drain

139.play: Upon his face an ocean of salt tears,

140.play: To tell my love unto his dumb deaf trunk,

141.play: And with my fingers feel his hand unfeeling:

142.play: But all in vain are these mean obsequies;

143.play: And to survey his dead and earthly image,

144.play: What were it but to make my sorrow greater?

145.play: WARWICK: Come hither, gracious sovereign, view this body.

146.play: KING HENRY VI: That is to see how deep my grave is made;

147.play: For with his soul fled all my worldly solace,

148.play: For seeing him I see my life in death.

149.play: WARWICK: As surely as my soul intends to live

150.play: With that dread King that took our state upon him

151.play: To free us from his father's wrathful curse,

152.play: I do believe that violent hands were laid

153.play: Upon the life of this thrice-famed duke.

154.play: SUFFOLK: A dreadful oath, sworn with a solemn tongue!

155.play: What instance gives Lord Warwick for his vow?

156.play: WARWICK: See how the blood is settled in his face.

157.play: Oft have I seen a timely-parted ghost,

158.play: Of ashy semblance, meagre, pale and bloodless,

159.play: Being all descended to the labouring heart;

160.play: Who, in the conflict that it holds with death,

161.play: Attracts the same for aidance 'gainst the enemy;

162.play: Which with the heart there cools and ne'er returneth

163.play: To blush and beautify the cheek again.

164.play: But see, his face is black and full of blood,

165.play: His eye-balls further out than when he lived,

166.play: Staring full ghastly like a strangled man;

167.play: His hair uprear'd, his nostrils stretched with struggling;

168.play: His hands abroad display'd, as one that grasp'd

169.play: And tugg'd for life and was by strength subdued:

170.play: Look, on the sheets his hair you see, is sticking;

171.play: His well-proportion'd beard made rough and rugged,

172.play: Like to the summer's corn by tempest lodged.

173.play: It cannot be but he was murder'd here;

174.play: The least of all these signs were probable.

175.play: SUFFOLK: Why, Warwick, who should do the duke to death?

176.play: Myself and Beaufort had him in protection;

177.play: And we, I hope, sir, are no murderers.

178.play: WARWICK: But both of you were vow'd Duke Humphrey's foes,

179.play: And you, forsooth, had the good duke to keep:

180.play: 'Tis like you would not feast him like a friend;

181.play: And 'tis well seen he found an enemy.

182.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Then you, belike, suspect these noblemen

183.play: As guilty of Duke Humphrey's timeless death.

184.play: WARWICK: Who finds the heifer dead and bleeding fresh

185.play: And sees fast by a butcher with an axe,

186.play: But will suspect 'twas he that made the slaughter?

187.play: Who finds the partridge in the puttock's nest,

188.play: But may imagine how the bird was dead,

189.play: Although the kite soar with unbloodied beak?

190.play: Even so suspicious is this tragedy.

191.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Are you the butcher, Suffolk? Where's your knife?

192.play: Is Beaufort term'd a kite? Where are his talons?

193.play: SUFFOLK: I wear no knife to slaughter sleeping men;

194.play: But here's a vengeful sword, rusted with ease,

195.play: That shall be scoured in his rancorous heart

196.play: That slanders me with murder's crimson badge.

197.play: Say, if thou darest, proud Lord of Warwick-shire,

198.play: That I am faulty in Duke Humphrey's death.

199.play: WARWICK: What dares not Warwick, if false Suffolk dare him?

200.play: QUEEN MARGARET: He dares not calm his contumelious spirit

201.play: Nor cease to be an arrogant controller,

202.play: Though Suffolk dare him twenty thousand times.

203.play: WARWICK: Madam, be still; with reverence may I say;

204.play: For every word you speak in his behalf

205.play: Is slander to your royal dignity.

206.play: SUFFOLK: Blunt-witted lord, ignoble in demeanor!

207.play: If ever lady wrong'd her lord so much,

208.play: Thy mother took into her blameful bed

209.play: Some stern untutor'd churl, and noble stock

210.play: Was graft with crab-tree slip; whose fruit thou art,

211.play: And never of the Nevils' noble race.

212.play: WARWICK: But that the guilt of murder bucklers thee

213.play: And I should rob the deathsman of his fee,

214.play: Quitting thee thereby of ten thousand shames,

215.play: And that my sovereign's presence makes me mild,

216.play: I would, false murderous coward, on thy knee

217.play: Make thee beg pardon for thy passed speech,

218.play: And say it was thy mother that thou meant'st

219.play: That thou thyself was born in bastardy;

220.play: And after all this fearful homage done,

221.play: Give thee thy hire and send thy soul to hell,

223.play: SUFFOLK: Thou shall be waking well I shed thy blood,

224.play: If from this presence thou darest go with me.

225.play: WARWICK: Away even now, or I will drag thee hence:

226.play: Unworthy though thou art, I'll cope with thee

227.play: And do some service to Duke Humphrey's ghost.

228.play: KING HENRY VI: What stronger breastplate than a heart untainted!

229.play: Thrice is he armed that hath his quarrel just,

230.play: And he but naked, though lock'd up in steel

231.play: Whose conscience with injustice is corrupted.

232.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What noise is this?

233.play: KING HENRY VI: Why, how now, lords! your wrathful weapons drawn

234.play: Here in our presence! dare you be so bold?

235.play: Why, what tumultuous clamour have we here?

236.play: SUFFOLK: The traitorous Warwick with the men of Bury

237.play: Set all upon me, mighty sovereign.

238.play: SALISBURY: [To the Commons, entering]  Sirs, stand apart;

239.play: the king shall know your mind.

240.play: Dread lord, the commons send you word by me,

241.play: Unless Lord Suffolk straight be done to death,

243.play: They will by violence tear him from your palace

244.play: And torture him with grievous lingering death.

245.play: They say, by him the good Duke Humphrey died;

246.play: They say, in him they fear your highness' death;

247.play: And mere instinct of love and loyalty,

248.play: Free from a stubborn opposite intent,

249.play: As being thought to contradict your liking,

250.play: Makes them thus forward in his banishment.

251.play: They say, in care of your most royal person,

252.play: That if your highness should intend to sleep

253.play: And charge that no man should disturb your rest

254.play: In pain of your dislike or pain of death,

255.play: Yet, notwithstanding such a strait edict,

256.play: Were there a serpent seen, with forked tongue,

257.play: That slily glided towards your majesty,

258.play: It were but necessary you were waked,

259.play: Lest, being suffer'd in that harmful slumber,

260.play: The mortal worm might make the sleep eternal;

261.play: And therefore do they cry, though you forbid,

262.play: That they will guard you, whether you will or no,

263.play: From such fell serpents as false Suffolk is,

264.play: With whose envenomed and fatal sting,

265.play: Your loving uncle, twenty times his worth,

266.play: They say, is shamefully bereft of life.

267.play: SUFFOLK: 'Tis like the commons, rude unpolish'd hinds,

268.play: Could send such message to their sovereign:

269.play: But you, my lord, were glad to be employ'd,

270.play: To show how quaint an orator you are:

271.play: But all the honour Salisbury hath won

272.play: Is, that he was the lord ambassador

273.play: Sent from a sort of tinkers to the king.

274.play: KING HENRY VI: Go, Salisbury, and tell them all from me.

275.play: I thank them for their tender loving care;

276.play: And had I not been cited so by them,

277.play: Yet did I purpose as they do entreat;

278.play: For, sure, my thoughts do hourly prophesy

279.play: Mischance unto my state by Suffolk's means:

280.play: And therefore, by His majesty I swear,

281.play: Whose far unworthy deputy I am,

282.play: He shall not breathe infection in this air

283.play: But three days longer, on the pain of death.

284.play: QUEEN MARGARET: O Henry, let me plead for gentle Suffolk!

285.play: KING HENRY VI: Ungentle queen, to call him gentle Suffolk!

286.play: No more, I say: if thou dost plead for him,

287.play: Thou wilt but add increase unto my wrath.

288.play: Had I but said, I would have kept my word,

289.play: But when I swear, it is irrevocable.

290.play: If, after three days' space, thou here be'st found

291.play: On any ground that I am ruler of,

292.play: The world shall not be ransom for thy life.

293.play: Come, Warwick, come, good Warwick, go with me;

294.play: I have great matters to impart to thee.

295.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Mischance and sorrow go along with you!

297.play: Be playfellows to keep you company!

298.play: There's two of you; the devil make a third!

299.play: And threefold vengeance tend upon your steps!

300.play: SUFFOLK: Cease, gentle queen, these execrations,

301.play: And let thy Suffolk take his heavy leave.

302.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Fie, coward woman and soft-hearted wretch!

303.play: Hast thou not spirit to curse thine enemy?

304.play: SUFFOLK: A plague upon them! wherefore should I curse them?

305.play: Would curses kill, as doth the mandrake's groan,

306.play: I would invent as bitter-searching terms,

307.play: As curst, as harsh and horrible to hear,

308.play: Deliver'd strongly through my fixed teeth,

309.play: With full as many signs of deadly hate,

310.play: As lean-faced Envy in her loathsome cave:

311.play: My tongue should stumble in mine earnest words;

312.play: Mine eyes should sparkle like the beaten flint;

313.play: Mine hair be fixed on end, as one distract;

314.play: Ay, every joint should seem to curse and ban:

315.play: And even now my burthen'd heart would break,

316.play: Should I not curse them. Poison be their drink!

317.play: Gall, worse than gall, the daintiest that they taste!

318.play: Their sweetest shade a grove of cypress trees!

320.play: Their softest touch as smart as lizards' sting!

321.play: Their music frightful as the serpent's hiss,

322.play: And boding screech-owls make the concert full!

323.play: All the foul terrors in dark-seated hell--

324.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Enough, sweet Suffolk; thou torment'st thyself;

325.play: And these dread curses, like the sun 'gainst glass,

326.play: Or like an overcharged gun, recoil,

327.play: And turn the force of them upon thyself.

328.play: SUFFOLK: You bade me ban, and will you bid me leave?

329.play: Now, by the ground that I am banish'd from,

330.play: Well could I curse away a winter's night,

331.play: Though standing naked on a mountain top,

332.play: Where biting cold would never let grass grow,

333.play: And think it but a minute spent in sport.

334.play: QUEEN MARGARET: O, let me entreat thee cease. Give me thy hand,

335.play: That I may dew it with my mournful tears;

336.play: Nor let the rain of heaven wet this place,

337.play: To wash away my woful monuments.

338.play: O, could this kiss be printed in thy hand,

339.play: That thou mightst think upon these by the seal,

340.play: Through whom a thousand sighs are breathed for thee!

341.play: So, get thee gone, that I may know my grief;

342.play: 'Tis but surmised whiles thou art standing by,

343.play: As one that surfeits thinking on a want.

344.play: I will repeal thee, or, be well assured,

346.play: And banished I am, if but from thee.

347.play: Go; speak not to me; even now be gone.

348.play: O, go not yet! Even thus two friends condemn'd

349.play: Embrace and kiss and take ten thousand leaves,

350.play: Loather a hundred times to part than die.

351.play: Yet now farewell; and farewell life with thee!

352.play: SUFFOLK: Thus is poor Suffolk ten times banished;

353.play: Once by the king, and three times thrice by thee.

354.play: 'Tis not the land I care for, wert thou thence;

356.play: So Suffolk had thy heavenly company:

357.play: For where thou art, there is the world itself,

358.play: With every several pleasure in the world,

359.play: And where thou art not, desolation.

360.play: I can no more: live thou to joy thy life;

361.play: Myself no joy in nought but that thou livest.

362.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Wither goes Vaux so fast? what news, I prithee?

363.play: VAUX: To signify unto his majesty

364.play: That Cardinal Beaufort is at point of death;

365.play: For suddenly a grievous sickness took him,

367.play: Blaspheming God and cursing men on earth.

377.play: Omitting Suffolk's exile, my soul's treasure?

51.play: SAY: Nothing but this; 'tis 'bona terra, mala gens.'

52.play: CADE: Away with him, away with him! he speaks Latin.

54.play: Kent, in the Commentaries Caesar writ,

55.play: Is term'd the civil'st place of this isle:

56.play: Sweet is the country, because full of riches;

74.play: SAY: Great men have reaching hands: oft have I struck

89.play: Are my chests fill'd up with extorted gold?

90.play: Is my apparel sumptuous to behold?

91.play: Whom have I injured, that ye seek my death?

92.play: These hands are free from guiltless bloodshedding,

93.play: This breast from harbouring foul deceitful thoughts.

95.play: CADE: [Aside]  I feel remorse in myself with his words;

104.play: SAY: Ah, countrymen! if when you make your prayers,

106.play: How would it fare with your departed souls?

107.play: And therefore yet relent, and save my life.

108.play: CADE: Away with him! and do as I command ye.

109.play: The proudest peer in the realm shall not wear a head

110.play: on his shoulders, unless he pay me tribute; there

111.play: shall not a maid be married, but she shall pay to me

112.play: her maidenhead ere they have it: men shall hold of

113.play: me in capite; and we charge and command that their

114.play: wives be as free as heart can wish or tongue can tell.

122.play: some more towns in France. Soldiers, defer the
