1.play: [Alarum and retreat. Enter CADE and all his rabblement]

2.play: CADE: Up Fish Street! down Saint Magnus' Corner! Kill

3.play: and knock down! throw them into Thames!

4.play: What noise is this I hear? Dare any be so bold to

5.play: sound retreat or parley, when I command them kill?

6.play: BUCKINGHAM: Ay, here they be that dare and will disturb thee:

7.play: Know, Cade, we come ambassadors from the king

8.play: Unto the commons whom thou hast misled;

9.play: And here pronounce free pardon to them all

10.play: That will forsake thee and go home in peace.

11.play: CLIFFORD: What say ye, countrymen? will ye relent,

12.play: And yield to mercy whilst 'tis offer'd you;

13.play: Or let a rebel lead you to your deaths?

14.play: Who loves the king and will embrace his pardon,

15.play: Fling up his cap, and say 'God save his majesty!'

16.play: Who hateth him and honours not his father,

17.play: Henry the Fifth, that made all France to quake,

18.play: Shake he his weapon at us and pass by.

19.play: ALL: God save the king! God save the king!

20.play: CADE: What, Buckingham and Clifford, are ye so brave? And

21.play: you, base peasants, do ye believe him? will you

22.play: needs be hanged with your pardons about your necks?

23.play: Hath my sword therefore broke through London gates,

24.play: that you should leave me at the White Hart in

25.play: Southwark? I thought ye would never have given out

26.play: these arms till you had recovered your ancient

27.play: freedom: but you are all recreants and dastards,

28.play: and delight to live in slavery to the nobility. Let

29.play: them break your backs with burthens, take your

30.play: houses over your heads, ravish your wives and

31.play: daughters before your faces: for me, I will make

32.play: shift for one; and so, God's curse light upon you

33.play: all!

34.play: ALL: We'll follow Cade, we'll follow Cade!

35.play: CLIFFORD: Is Cade the son of Henry the Fifth,

36.play: That thus you do exclaim you'll go with him?

37.play: Will he conduct you through the heart of France,

38.play: And make the meanest of you earls and dukes?

39.play: Alas, he hath no home, no place to fly to;

40.play: Nor knows he how to live but by the spoil,

41.play: Unless by robbing of your friends and us.

42.play: Were't not a shame, that whilst you live at jar,

43.play: The fearful French, whom you late vanquished,

44.play: Should make a start o'er seas and vanquish you?

45.play: Methinks already in this civil broil

46.play: I see them lording it in London streets,

48.play: Better ten thousand base-born Cades miscarry

49.play: Than you should stoop unto a Frenchman's mercy.

50.play: To France, to France, and get what you have lost;

51.play: Spare England, for it is your native coast;

52.play: Henry hath money, you are strong and manly;

53.play: God on our side, doubt not of victory.

54.play: ALL: A Clifford! a Clifford! we'll follow the king and Clifford.

55.play: CADE: Was ever feather so lightly blown to and fro as this

56.play: multitude? The name of Henry the Fifth hales them

57.play: to an hundred mischiefs, and makes them leave me

58.play: desolate. I see them lay their heads together to

59.play: surprise me. My sword make way for me, for here is

60.play: no staying. In despite of the devils and hell, have

61.play: through the very middest of you? and heavens and

62.play: honour be witness, that no want of resolution in me.

63.play: but only my followers' base and ignominious

64.play: treasons, makes me betake me to my heels.

65.play: BUCKINGHAM: What, is he fled? Go some, and follow him;

66.play: And he that brings his head unto the king

67.play: Shall have a thousand crowns for his reward.

68.play: Follow me, soldiers: we'll devise a mean

69.play: To reconcile you all unto the king.

1.play: KING HENRY VI: Was ever king that joy'd an earthly throne,

2.play: And could command no more content than I?

3.play: No sooner was I crept out of my cradle

4.play: But I was made a king, at nine months old.

5.play: Was never subject long'd to be a king

6.play: As I do long and wish to be a subject.

7.play: BUCKINGHAM: Health and glad tidings to your majesty!

8.play: KING HENRY VI: Why, Buckingham, is the traitor Cade surprised?

9.play: Or is he but retired to make him strong?

10.play: CLIFFORD: He is fled, my lord, and all his powers do yield;

11.play: And humbly thus, with halters on their necks,

12.play: Expect your highness' doom of life or death.

13.play: KING HENRY VI: Then, heaven, set ope thy everlasting gates,

14.play: To entertain my vows of thanks and praise!

15.play: Soldiers, this day have you redeemed your lives,

16.play: And show'd how well you love your prince and country:

17.play: Continue still in this so good a mind,

18.play: And Henry, though he be infortunate,

19.play: Assure yourselves, will never be unkind:

20.play: And so, with thanks and pardon to you all,

21.play: I do dismiss you to your several countries.

22.play: ALL: God save the king! God save the king!

23.play: KING HENRY VI: Thus stands my state, 'twixt Cade and York distress'd.

24.play: Like to a ship that, having 'scaped a tempest,

25.play: Is straightway calm'd and boarded with a pirate:

26.play: But now is Cade driven back, his men dispersed;

27.play: And now is York in arms to second him.

28.play: I pray thee, Buckingham, go and meet him,

29.play: And ask him what's the reason of these arms.

30.play: Tell him I'll send Duke Edmund to the Tower;

31.play: And, Somerset, we'll commit thee thither,

32.play: Until his army be dismiss'd from him.

33.play: SOMERSET: My lord,

34.play: I'll yield myself to prison willingly,

35.play: Or unto death, to do my country good.

36.play: KING HENRY VI: In any case, be not too rough in terms;

37.play: For he is fierce and cannot brook hard language.

38.play: BUCKINGHAM: I will, my lord; and doubt not so to deal

39.play: As all things shall redound unto your good.

40.play: KING HENRY VI: Come, wife, let's in, and learn to govern better;

41.play: For yet may England curse my wretched reign.

1.play: [Enter CADE]

2.play: CADE: Fie on ambition! fie on myself, that have a sword,

3.play: and yet am ready to famish! These five days have I

4.play: hid me in these woods and durst not peep out, for

5.play: all the country is laid for me; but now am I so

6.play: hungry that if I might have a lease of my life for a

7.play: thousand years I could stay no longer. Wherefore,

8.play: on a brick wall have I climbed into this garden, to

9.play: see if I can eat grass, or pick a sallet another

10.play: while, which is not amiss to cool a man's stomach

11.play: this hot weather. And I think this word 'sallet'

12.play: was born to do me good: for many a time, but for a

13.play: sallet, my brainpan had been cleft with a brown

14.play: bill; and many a time, when I have been dry and

15.play: bravely marching, it hath served me instead of a

16.play: quart pot to drink in; and now the word 'sallet'

17.play: must serve me to feed on.

18.play: IDEN: Lord, who would live turmoiled in the court,

19.play: And may enjoy such quiet walks as these?

20.play: This small inheritance my father left me

21.play: Contenteth me, and worth a monarchy.

22.play: I seek not to wax great by others' waning,

23.play: Or gather wealth, I care not, with what envy:

24.play: Sufficeth that I have maintains my state

25.play: And sends the poor well pleased from my gate.

26.play: CADE: Here's the lord of the soil come to seize me for a

27.play: stray, for entering his fee-simple without leave.

28.play: Ah, villain, thou wilt betray me, and get a thousand

29.play: crowns of the king carrying my head to him: but

30.play: I'll make thee eat iron like an ostrich, and swallow

31.play: my sword like a great pin, ere thou and I part.

32.play: IDEN: Why, rude companion, whatsoe'er thou be,

33.play: I know thee not; why, then, should I betray thee?

34.play: Is't not enough to break into my garden,

35.play: And, like a thief, to come to rob my grounds,

36.play: Climbing my walls in spite of me the owner,

37.play: But thou wilt brave me with these saucy terms?

38.play: CADE: Brave thee! ay, by the best blood that ever was

39.play: broached, and beard thee too. Look on me well: I

40.play: have eat no meat these five days; yet, come thou and

41.play: thy five men, and if I do not leave you all as dead

42.play: as a doornail, I pray God I may never eat grass more.

43.play: IDEN: Nay, it shall ne'er be said, while England stands,

44.play: That Alexander Iden, an esquire of Kent,

45.play: Took odds to combat a poor famish'd man.

46.play: Oppose thy steadfast-gazing eyes to mine,

47.play: See if thou canst outface me with thy looks:

48.play: Set limb to limb, and thou art far the lesser;

49.play: Thy hand is but a finger to my fist,

50.play: Thy leg a stick compared with this truncheon;

51.play: My foot shall fight with all the strength thou hast;

52.play: And if mine arm be heaved in the air,

53.play: Thy grave is digg'd already in the earth.

54.play: As for words, whose greatness answers words,

55.play: Let this my sword report what speech forbears.

56.play: CADE: By my valour, the most complete champion that ever I

57.play: heard! Steel, if thou turn the edge, or cut not out

58.play: the burly-boned clown in chines of beef ere thou

59.play: sleep in thy sheath, I beseech God on my knees thou

61.play: O, I am slain! famine and no other hath slain me:

62.play: let ten thousand devils come against me, and give me

63.play: but the ten meals I have lost, and I'll defy them

64.play: all. Wither, garden; and be henceforth a

65.play: burying-place to all that do dwell in this house,

66.play: because the unconquered soul of Cade is fled.

67.play: IDEN: Is't Cade that I have slain, that monstrous traitor?

68.play: Sword, I will hollow thee for this thy deed,

69.play: And hang thee o'er my tomb when I am dead:

70.play: Ne'er shall this blood be wiped from thy point;

71.play: But thou shalt wear it as a herald's coat,

72.play: To emblaze the honour that thy master got.

73.play: CADE: Iden, farewell, and be proud of thy victory. Tell

74.play: Kent from me, she hath lost her best man, and exhort

75.play: all the world to be cowards; for I, that never

76.play: feared any, am vanquished by famine, not by valour.

77.play: IDEN: How much thou wrong'st me, heaven be my judge.

78.play: Die, damned wretch, the curse of her that bare thee;

79.play: And as I thrust thy body in with my sword,

80.play: So wish I, I might thrust thy soul to hell.

81.play: Hence will I drag thee headlong by the heels

82.play: Unto a dunghill which shall be thy grave,

83.play: And there cut off thy most ungracious head;

84.play: Which I will bear in triumph to the king,

85.play: Leaving thy trunk for crows to feed upon.

1.play: [Enter YORK, and his army of Irish, with drum and colours]

2.play: YORK: From Ireland thus comes York to claim his right,

3.play: And pluck the crown from feeble Henry's head:

4.play: Ring, bells, aloud; burn, bonfires, clear and bright,

5.play: To entertain great England's lawful king.

6.play: Ah! sancta majestas, who would not buy thee dear?

7.play: Let them obey that know not how to rule;

8.play: This hand was made to handle naught but gold.

9.play: I cannot give due action to my words,

10.play: Except a sword or sceptre balance it:

11.play: A sceptre shall it have, have I a soul,

12.play: On which I'll toss the flower-de-luce of France.

13.play: Whom have we here? Buckingham, to disturb me?

14.play: The king hath sent him, sure: I must dissemble.

15.play: BUCKINGHAM: York, if thou meanest well, I greet thee well.

16.play: YORK: Humphrey of Buckingham, I accept thy greeting.

17.play: Art thou a messenger, or come of pleasure?

18.play: BUCKINGHAM: A messenger from Henry, our dread liege,

19.play: To know the reason of these arms in peace;

20.play: Or why thou, being a subject as I am,

21.play: Against thy oath and true allegiance sworn,

22.play: Should raise so great a power without his leave,

23.play: Or dare to bring thy force so near the court.

24.play: YORK: [Aside]  Scarce can I speak, my choler is so great:

25.play: O, I could hew up rocks and fight with flint,

26.play: I am so angry at these abject terms;

27.play: And now, like Ajax Telamonius,

28.play: On sheep or oxen could I spend my fury.

29.play: I am far better born than is the king,

30.play: More like a king, more kingly in my thoughts:

31.play: But I must make fair weather yet a while,

32.play: Till Henry be more weak and I more strong,--

33.play: Buckingham, I prithee, pardon me,

34.play: That I have given no answer all this while;

35.play: My mind was troubled with deep melancholy.

36.play: The cause why I have brought this army hither

37.play: Is to remove proud Somerset from the king,

38.play: Seditious to his grace and to the state.

39.play: BUCKINGHAM: That is too much presumption on thy part:

40.play: But if thy arms be to no other end,

41.play: The king hath yielded unto thy demand:

42.play: The Duke of Somerset is in the Tower.

43.play: YORK: Upon thine honour, is he prisoner?

44.play: BUCKINGHAM: Upon mine honour, he is prisoner.

45.play: YORK: Then, Buckingham, I do dismiss my powers.

46.play: Soldiers, I thank you all; disperse yourselves;

47.play: Meet me to-morrow in St. George's field,

48.play: You shall have pay and every thing you wish.

49.play: And let my sovereign, virtuous Henry,

50.play: Command my eldest son, nay, all my sons,

51.play: As pledges of my fealty and love;

52.play: I'll send them all as willing as I live:

53.play: Lands, goods, horse, armour, any thing I have,

54.play: Is his to use, so Somerset may die.

55.play: BUCKINGHAM: York, I commend this kind submission:

56.play: We twain will go into his highness' tent.

57.play: KING HENRY VI: Buckingham, doth York intend no harm to us,

58.play: That thus he marcheth with thee arm in arm?

59.play: YORK: In all submission and humility

60.play: York doth present himself unto your highness.

61.play: KING HENRY VI: Then what intends these forces thou dost bring?

62.play: YORK: To heave the traitor Somerset from hence,

63.play: And fight against that monstrous rebel Cade,

64.play: Who since I heard to be discomfited.

65.play: IDEN: If one so rude and of so mean condition

66.play: May pass into the presence of a king,

67.play: Lo, I present your grace a traitor's head,

68.play: The head of Cade, whom I in combat slew.

69.play: KING HENRY VI: The head of Cade! Great God, how just art Thou!

70.play: O, let me view his visage, being dead,

71.play: That living wrought me such exceeding trouble.

72.play: Tell me, my friend, art thou the man that slew him?

73.play: IDEN: I was, an't like your majesty.

74.play: KING HENRY VI: How art thou call'd? and what is thy degree?

75.play: IDEN: Alexander Iden, that's my name;

76.play: A poor esquire of Kent, that loves his king.

77.play: BUCKINGHAM: So please it you, my lord, 'twere not amiss

78.play: He were created knight for his good service.

79.play: KING HENRY VI: Iden, kneel down.

81.play: We give thee for reward a thousand marks,

82.play: And will that thou henceforth attend on us.

83.play: IDEN: May Iden live to merit such a bounty.

84.play: And never live but true unto his liege!

85.play: KING HENRY VI: See, Buckingham, Somerset comes with the queen:

86.play: Go, bid her hide him quickly from the duke.

87.play: QUEEN MARGARET: For thousand Yorks he shall not hide his head,

88.play: But boldly stand and front him to his face.

89.play: YORK: How now! is Somerset at liberty?

90.play: Then, York, unloose thy long-imprison'd thoughts,

91.play: And let thy tongue be equal with thy heart.

92.play: Shall I endure the sight of Somerset?

93.play: False king! why hast thou broken faith with me,

94.play: Knowing how hardly I can brook abuse?

95.play: King did I call thee? no, thou art not king,

96.play: Not fit to govern and rule multitudes,

97.play: Which darest not, no, nor canst not rule a traitor.

98.play: That head of thine doth not become a crown;

99.play: Thy hand is made to grasp a palmer's staff,

100.play: And not to grace an awful princely sceptre.

101.play: That gold must round engirt these brows of mine,

102.play: Whose smile and frown, like to Achilles' spear,

103.play: Is able with the change to kill and cure.

104.play: Here is a hand to hold a sceptre up

105.play: And with the same to act controlling laws.

106.play: Give place: by heaven, thou shalt rule no more

107.play: O'er him whom heaven created for thy ruler.

108.play: SOMERSET: O monstrous traitor! I arrest thee, York,

109.play: Of capital treason 'gainst the king and crown;

110.play: Obey, audacious traitor; kneel for grace.

111.play: YORK: Wouldst have me kneel? first let me ask of these,

112.play: If they can brook I bow a knee to man.

113.play: Sirrah, call in my sons to be my bail;

114.play: I know, ere they will have me go to ward,

115.play: They'll pawn their swords for my enfranchisement.

116.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Call hither Clifford! bid him come amain,

117.play: To say if that the bastard boys of York

118.play: Shall be the surety for their traitor father.

120.play: Outcast of Naples, England's bloody scourge!

121.play: The sons of York, thy betters in their birth,

122.play: Shall be their father's bail; and bane to those

123.play: That for my surety will refuse the boys!

124.play: See where they come: I'll warrant they'll

126.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And here comes Clifford to deny their bail.

127.play: CLIFFORD: Health and all happiness to my lord the king!

128.play: YORK: I thank thee, Clifford: say, what news with thee?

129.play: Nay, do not fright us with an angry look;

130.play: We are thy sovereign, Clifford, kneel again;

131.play: For thy mistaking so, we pardon thee.

132.play: CLIFFORD: This is my king, York, I do not mistake;

133.play: But thou mistakest me much to think I do:

134.play: To Bedlam with him! is the man grown mad?

135.play: KING HENRY VI: Ay, Clifford; a bedlam and ambitious humour

136.play: Makes him oppose himself against his king.

137.play: CLIFFORD: He is a traitor; let him to the Tower,

138.play: And chop away that factious pate of his.

139.play: QUEEN MARGARET: He is arrested, but will not obey;

140.play: His sons, he says, shall give their words for him.

142.play: EDWARD: Ay, noble father, if our words will serve.

143.play: RICHARD: And if words will not, then our weapons shall.

144.play: CLIFFORD: Why, what a brood of traitors have we here!

145.play: YORK: Look in a glass, and call thy image so:

146.play: I am thy king, and thou a false-heart traitor.

147.play: Call hither to the stake my two brave bears,

148.play: That with the very shaking of their chains

149.play: They may astonish these fell-lurking curs:

150.play: Bid Salisbury and Warwick come to me.

151.play: CLIFFORD: Are these thy bears? we'll bait thy bears to death.

152.play: And manacle the bear-ward in their chains,

153.play: If thou darest bring them to the baiting place.

154.play: RICHARD: Oft have I seen a hot o'erweening cur

155.play: Run back and bite, because he was withheld;

156.play: Who, being suffer'd with the bear's fell paw,

157.play: Hath clapp'd his tail between his legs and cried:

158.play: And such a piece of service will you do,

159.play: If you oppose yourselves to match Lord Warwick.

160.play: CLIFFORD: Hence, heap of wrath, foul indigested lump,

161.play: As crooked in thy manners as thy shape!

162.play: YORK: Nay, we shall heat you thoroughly anon.

163.play: CLIFFORD: Take heed, lest by your heat you burn yourselves.

164.play: KING HENRY VI: Why, Warwick, hath thy knee forgot to bow?

165.play: Old Salisbury, shame to thy silver hair,

166.play: Thou mad misleader of thy brain-sick son!

167.play: What, wilt thou on thy death-bed play the ruffian,

168.play: And seek for sorrow with thy spectacles?

169.play: O, where is faith? O, where is loyalty?

170.play: If it be banish'd from the frosty head,

171.play: Where shall it find a harbour in the earth?

172.play: Wilt thou go dig a grave to find out war,

173.play: And shame thine honourable age with blood?

174.play: Why art thou old, and want'st experience?

175.play: Or wherefore dost abuse it, if thou hast it?

176.play: For shame! in duty bend thy knee to me

177.play: That bows unto the grave with mickle age.

178.play: SALISBURY: My lord, I have consider'd with myself

179.play: The title of this most renowned duke;

180.play: And in my conscience do repute his grace

181.play: The rightful heir to England's royal seat.

182.play: KING HENRY VI: Hast thou not sworn allegiance unto me?

184.play: KING HENRY VI: Canst thou dispense with heaven for such an oath?

185.play: SALISBURY: It is great sin to swear unto a sin,

186.play: But greater sin to keep a sinful oath.

187.play: Who can be bound by any solemn vow

188.play: To do a murderous deed, to rob a man,

189.play: To force a spotless virgin's chastity,

190.play: To reave the orphan of his patrimony,

191.play: To wring the widow from her custom'd right,

192.play: And have no other reason for this wrong

193.play: But that he was bound by a solemn oath?

194.play: QUEEN MARGARET: A subtle traitor needs no sophister.

195.play: KING HENRY VI: Call Buckingham, and bid him arm himself.

196.play: YORK: Call Buckingham, and all the friends thou hast,

197.play: I am resolved for death or dignity.

198.play: CLIFFORD: The first I warrant thee, if dreams prove true.

199.play: WARWICK: You were best to go to bed and dream again,

200.play: To keep thee from the tempest of the field.

201.play: CLIFFORD: I am resolved to bear a greater storm

202.play: Than any thou canst conjure up to-day;

203.play: And that I'll write upon thy burgonet,

204.play: Might I but know thee by thy household badge.

205.play: WARWICK: Now, by my father's badge, old Nevil's crest,

206.play: The rampant bear chain'd to the ragged staff,

207.play: This day I'll wear aloft my burgonet,

208.play: As on a mountain top the cedar shows

209.play: That keeps his leaves in spite of any storm,

210.play: Even to affright thee with the view thereof.

211.play: CLIFFORD: And from thy burgonet I'll rend thy bear

212.play: And tread it under foot with all contempt,

213.play: Despite the bear-ward that protects the bear.

214.play: YOUNG CLIFFORD: And so to arms, victorious father,

215.play: To quell the rebels and their complices.

216.play: RICHARD: Fie! charity, for shame! speak not in spite,

217.play: For you shall sup with Jesu Christ to-night.

218.play: YOUNG CLIFFORD: Foul stigmatic, that's more than thou canst tell.

219.play: RICHARD: If not in heaven, you'll surely sup in hell.

1.play: [Alarums to the battle. Enter WARWICK]

2.play: WARWICK: Clifford of Cumberland, 'tis Warwick calls:

3.play: And if thou dost not hide thee from the bear,

4.play: Now, when the angry trumpet sounds alarum

5.play: And dead men's cries do fill the empty air,

6.play: Clifford, I say, come forth and fight with me:

7.play: Proud northern lord, Clifford of Cumberland,

8.play: Warwick is hoarse with calling thee to arms.

9.play: How now, my noble lord? what, all afoot?

10.play: YORK: The deadly-handed Clifford slew my steed,

11.play: But match to match I have encounter'd him

12.play: And made a prey for carrion kites and crows

13.play: Even of the bonny beast he loved so well.

14.play: WARWICK: Of one or both of us the time is come.

15.play: YORK: Hold, Warwick, seek thee out some other chase,

16.play: For I myself must hunt this deer to death.

17.play: WARWICK: Then, nobly, York; 'tis for a crown thou fight'st.

18.play: As I intend, Clifford, to thrive to-day,

19.play: It grieves my soul to leave thee unassail'd.

20.play: CLIFFORD: What seest thou in me, York? why dost thou pause?

21.play: YORK: With thy brave bearing should I be in love,

22.play: But that thou art so fast mine enemy.

23.play: CLIFFORD: Nor should thy prowess want praise and esteem,

24.play: But that 'tis shown ignobly and in treason.

25.play: YORK: So let it help me now against thy sword

26.play: As I in justice and true right express it.

27.play: CLIFFORD: My soul and body on the action both!

28.play: YORK: A dreadful lay! Address thee instantly.

29.play: CLIFFORD: La fin couronne les oeuvres.

30.play: YORK: Thus war hath given thee peace, for thou art still.

31.play: Peace with his soul, heaven, if it be thy will!

32.play: YOUNG CLIFFORD: Shame and confusion! all is on the rout;

33.play: Fear frames disorder, and disorder wounds

34.play: Where it should guard. O war, thou son of hell,

35.play: Whom angry heavens do make their minister

36.play: Throw in the frozen bosoms of our part

37.play: Hot coals of vengeance! Let no soldier fly.

38.play: He that is truly dedicate to war

39.play: Hath no self-love, nor he that loves himself

40.play: Hath not essentially but by circumstance

42.play: O, let the vile world end,

43.play: And the premised flames of the last day

45.play: Now let the general trumpet blow his blast,

47.play: To cease! Wast thou ordain'd, dear father,

48.play: To lose thy youth in peace, and to achieve

49.play: The silver livery of advised age,

50.play: And, in thy reverence and thy chair-days, thus

51.play: To die in ruffian battle? Even at this sight

52.play: My heart is turn'd to stone: and while 'tis mine,

53.play: It shall be stony. York not our old men spares;

54.play: No more will I their babes: tears virginal

55.play: Shall be to me even as the dew to fire,

56.play: And beauty that the tyrant oft reclaims

57.play: Shall to my flaming wrath be oil and flax.

58.play: Henceforth I will not have to do with pity:

59.play: Meet I an infant of the house of York,

60.play: Into as many gobbets will I cut it

61.play: As wild Medea young Absyrtus did:

62.play: In cruelty will I seek out my fame.

63.play: Come, thou new ruin of old Clifford's house:

64.play: As did AEneas old Anchises bear,

65.play: So bear I thee upon my manly shoulders;

66.play: But then AEneas bare a living load,

67.play: Nothing so heavy as these woes of mine.

69.play: For underneath an alehouse' paltry sign,

70.play: The Castle in Saint Alban's, Somerset

71.play: Hath made the wizard famous in his death.

72.play: Sword, hold thy temper; heart, be wrathful still:

73.play: Priests pray for enemies, but princes kill.

74.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Away, my lord! you are slow; for shame, away!

75.play: KING HENRY VI: Can we outrun the heavens? good Margaret, stay.

76.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What are you made of? you'll nor fight nor fly:

77.play: Now is it manhood, wisdom and defence,

78.play: To give the enemy way, and to secure us

79.play: By what we can, which can no more but fly.

80.play: If you be ta'en, we then should see the bottom

81.play: Of all our fortunes: but if we haply scape,

82.play: As well we may, if not through your neglect,

83.play: We shall to London get, where you are loved

84.play: And where this breach now in our fortunes made

86.play: YOUNG CLIFFORD: But that my heart's on future mischief set,

87.play: I would speak blasphemy ere bid you fly:

88.play: But fly you must; uncurable discomfit

89.play: Reigns in the hearts of all our present parts.

90.play: Away, for your relief! and we will live

91.play: To see their day and them our fortune give:

1.play: [Alarum. Retreat. Enter YORK, RICHARD, WARWICK, and Soldiers, with drum and colours]

2.play: YORK: Of Salisbury, who can report of him,

3.play: That winter lion, who in rage forgets

4.play: Aged contusions and all brush of time,

5.play: And, like a gallant in the brow of youth,

6.play: Repairs him with occasion? This happy day

7.play: Is not itself, nor have we won one foot,

8.play: If Salisbury be lost.

9.play: RICHARD: My noble father,

10.play: Three times to-day I holp him to his horse,

11.play: Three times bestrid him; thrice I led him off,

12.play: Persuaded him from any further act:

13.play: But still, where danger was, still there I met him;

14.play: And like rich hangings in a homely house,

15.play: So was his will in his old feeble body.

16.play: But, noble as he is, look where he comes.

17.play: SALISBURY: Now, by my sword, well hast thou fought to-day;

18.play: By the mass, so did we all. I thank you, Richard:

19.play: God knows how long it is I have to live;

20.play: And it hath pleased him that three times to-day

21.play: You have defended me from imminent death.

22.play: Well, lords, we have not got that which we have:

23.play: 'Tis not enough our foes are this time fled,

24.play: Being opposites of such repairing nature.

25.play: YORK: I know our safety is to follow them;

26.play: For, as I hear, the king is fled to London,

27.play: To call a present court of parliament.

28.play: Let us pursue him ere the writs go forth.

29.play: What says Lord Warwick? shall we after them?

30.play: WARWICK: After them! nay, before them, if we can.

31.play: Now, by my faith, lords, 'twas a glorious day:

32.play: Saint Alban's battle won by famous York

33.play: Shall be eternized in all age to come.

34.play: Sound drums and trumpets, and to London all:

35.play: And more such days as these to us befall!
